summary: Seventh U.S. president (1829-1837), nicknamed "Old Hickory" and, during his military career, "Sharp Knife." Rose to fame as a general in the War of 1812 (Battle of New Orleans) and First Seminole War before narrowly losing the 1824 election to John Quincy Adams in the "corrupt bargain," then winning in 1828. His presidency is defined by the Indian Removal Act (Trail of Tears), the "Bank War" against the Second Bank of the United States and its president Nicholas Biddle, the Specie Circular and resulting Panic of 1837, and the Nullification Crisis with South Carolina. Survived the first assassination attempt on a sitting president. Married to Rachel Jackson, whose contested divorce dogged his 1828 campaign.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How Jackson is identified in giveaways: the seventh U.S. president, first Democratic president, nicknamed "Old Hickory" for his toughness and "Sharp Knife" from his military campaigns. His estate, the Hermitage, sits near Nashville, Tennessee, and gives its name to the era's folklore and later historiography.

clues.clue: Seventh president of the United States
examples: name this seventh president of the United States, who was nicknamed "Old Hickory."
Calhoun served and later resigned as vice president under this 7th President of the United States.

clues.clue: Nicknamed "Old Hickory"
examples: name this president under whom the Indian Removal Act was passed [prompt on Old Hickory]
name this seventh president of the United States, who was nicknamed "Old Hickory."

clues.clue: Nicknamed "Sharp Knife" (from his Florida campaigns) and "King Mob"
examples: "Sharp Knife" and "King Mob" were nicknames of this president, empowered by Congress via the Force Bill
During his time in Florida, he became known as "Sharp Knife."

clues.clue: First president elected as a Democrat (founding figure of the modern Democratic Party)
examples: The Specie Circular was issued by this first Democratic president
He was the first Democratic president and had served as a general in the conquest of Florida and the War of 1812

clues.clue: Lived at the Hermitage plantation/estate near Nashville, Tennessee
examples: The subject of the Coffin Handbills, this man lived on a plantation known as the Hermitage.
This author drew from the archives at the Hermitage in his biography of Jackson, American Lion.

clues.clue: Gave his name to the term "Jacksonian democracy" describing the political era of the 1830s
examples: what U.S president succeeded by van Buren whose namesake "democracy" reigned during the 1830s?

works.name: Military Career: War of 1812 & First Seminole War
works.description: Jackson's pre-presidential fame rested entirely on his generalship: crushing the Creek Red Sticks, defending New Orleans against the British, and waging an unauthorized war in Spanish Florida that made him a national hero and a diplomatic liability.

clues.clue: As a general, defeated the Second British invasion force at the Battle of New Orleans (1815); Edward Pakenham died storming the earthworks after Thomas Mullins failed to provide ladders
examples: The "Coffin Handbills" accused Jackson of executing six of his militiamen at this battle. Edward Pakenham died storming the earthworks at this battle without the ladders that Thomas Mullins was supposed to provide.
this president, a hero of the Battle of New Orleans, declined to exert Federal force.

clues.clue: Accused in the "Coffin Handbills" (circulated by editor John Binns) of executing six militiamen
examples: The editor John Binns attacked this politician's supposed execution of six militiamen in the Coffin Handbill.
The "Coffin Handbills" accused Jackson of executing six of his militiamen at this battle.

clues.clue: Defeated William Weatherford's Red Sticks at the Battle of Horseshoe Bend during the Creek War
examples: This general executed Arbuthnot and Ambrister, two British citizens, for selling guns to the Indians, and defeating William Weatherford's Red Sticks at the Battle of Horseshoe Bend.

clues.clue: During the First Seminole War, court-martialed and executed British subjects Alexander Arbuthnot and Robert Ambrister for supplying arms to the Seminoles, sparking a diplomatic incident
examples: Jackson sparked a diplomatic incident with the British during the First Seminole War, when he court-martialed and executed these two British agents who were supplying the Indians.
Name this general who executed two British subjects during his warring against the Seminole tribes.

works.name: Elections of 1824 and 1828
works.description: Jackson won the popular and electoral plurality in 1824 but lost the presidency to John Quincy Adams when the contested election went to the House — the "corrupt bargain" that fueled his successful 1828 rematch, run amid a newly expanded (white male) electorate.

clues.clue: Won more electoral votes than John Quincy Adams in 1824 but lost the presidency in a House vote decided by the "corrupt bargain"
examples: Quincy Adams won the 1824 Presidential Election in a Congressional vote despite this war hero and Senator from Tennessee earning more electoral votes.
Name this general who narrowly lost to John Quincy Adams in the election of 1824 because of a "corrupt bargain" in the House of Representatives.

clues.clue: Defeated John Quincy Adams in the 1828 rematch, aided by newly expanded voter eligibility
examples: This man proceeded to defeat Quincy Adams in the Election of 1828.
Increased voter eligibility in the U.S. is widely credited with helping this president win the election of 1828.

clues.clue: 1828 campaign against him featured attacks on wife Rachel's bigamy (see Rachel Jackson section)
examples: Jackson received flak during the 1828 election because his wife, who had this first name, had never finished divorcing her first husband, so she was portrayed as being bigamous.

works.name: Indian Removal Act & Trail of Tears
works.description: Jackson's signature and most consequential domestic policy: the forced relocation of southeastern Native nations, especially the Cherokee, to west of the Mississippi. He also declined to enforce a Supreme Court ruling that favored Cherokee sovereignty.

clues.clue: Signed the Indian Removal Act (1830), leading to the forced relocation of the Cherokee and other tribes on the Trail of Tears
examples: name this president under whom the Indian Removal Act was passed, leading to the Trail of Tears.
Mankiller was a candidate to replace this president on the $20 bill. This president signed the Indian Removal Act, which forced the Cherokee to relocate to the Indian Territory.

clues.clue: After Worcester v. Georgia (1832) ruled states lacked jurisdiction over Cherokee land, reportedly said "John Marshall has made his decision; now let him enforce it" and declined to use federal force against Georgia
examples: This president said "John Marshall has made his decision; now let him enforce it" after a case in which state jurisdiction over Cherokee land was challenged.
When Georgia's governor at first refused to comply with the decision, this president, a hero of the Battle of New Orleans, declined to exert Federal force.

clues.clue: Under his administration, a man was arrested for collaborating with Cherokee leader Elias Boudinot on a Bible translation
examples: Under this president's administration, one man was arrested after collaborating with Elias Boudinot on a translation of the Bible.

clues.clue: Removal policy was opposed by Jeremiah Evarts, who wrote essays against it under the pseudonym "William Penn"
examples: A policy carried out by this president was opposed by Jeremiah Evarts, who circulated essays written under the pseudonym "William Penn" to lobby against it.

works.name: Bank War: Second Bank of the United States
works.description: Jackson's defining domestic political fight: he vetoed the recharter of the Second Bank of the United States, then had federal deposits withdrawn from it, clashing with the bank's president Nicholas Biddle. Opponents cast him as a would-be despot in political cartoons.

clues.clue: Vetoed the recharter of the Second Bank of the United States, fighting its president Nicholas Biddle in the "Bank War"
examples: This man fought with Nicholas Biddle over the renewal of the Second Bank of the United States' charter, and he removed many Indian tribes to the west of the Mississippi.
Sellers introduced the Market Revolution paradigm in a book about this U.S. president, who waged the so-called "Bank War" against the Second Bank of the United States.

clues.clue: Appointed Roger Taney as Treasury Secretary (after Thomas Hart Benton's Supreme Court rumor proved false, and after firing/reassigning two prior Treasury Secretaries) to withdraw federal deposits from the Bank
examples: After Thomas Hart Benton vehemently denied rumors that this president would appoint him to the Supreme Court, this president appointed Roger Taney instead.

Jackson alienated his Cabinet further by reassigning his first Secretary of the Treasury to Secretary of State, and outright firing the new Secretary of the Treasury, because both had refused to remove deposits from the Second Bank of the United States

clues.clue: Depicted as "King Andrew the First," trampling the Constitution and "internal improvements," wielding a scepter next to "of veto memory"
examples: Whigs circulated a cartoon of this man surrounded by phrases such as "HAD I BEEN CONSULTED" and "BORN TO COMMAND."
Name this satirical character, who wears a crown and fancy robes. The phrases "Had I been consulted" and "Born to command" surround the frame of the cartoon.

clues.clue: Depicted "slaying the many-headed monster" (the Bank) in another political cartoon; Thomas Nast later drew him riding a pig under "To the Victors Belong the Spoils"
examples: Another cartoon calls this man a "general" "slaying the many headed monster."
The Specie Circular was issued by this seventh US president, who slew the many-headed demon of the Second Bank of the US in a famous political cartoon.

works.name: Specie Circular & Panic of 1837
works.description: Jackson's 1836 executive order requiring gold or silver for public land purchases, meant to curb land speculation after the Bank War, but widely blamed for triggering the Panic of 1837 shortly after he left office.

clues.clue: Issued the Specie Circular (1836, carried out by Treasury Secretary Levi Woodbury), requiring gold or silver payment for public land purchases
examples: The Specie Circular was issued by this first Democratic president, whose other economic initiatives included dismantling the Second Bank of the United States.
Name this executive order that required anyone who purchased government land to pay in gold or silver.

clues.clue: The resulting devaluation of poorly-backed state bank paper currency helped cause the Panic of 1837
examples: The devaluation of poorly-backed paper currency caused by this president's abolition of the Second Bank of the United States led to a financial crisis.
the Panic of 1837 was caused by the policies of what US president

works.name: Nullification Crisis
works.description: South Carolina's attempt to nullify the "Tariff of Abominations" led Jackson to seek and sign the Force Bill authorizing federal enforcement, while Vice President John C. Calhoun broke with him and resigned.

clues.clue: Signed the Force Bill, authorizing federal enforcement of tariff collection against South Carolina, ending the Nullification Crisis
examples: This president authorized US marshals to arrest violators of federal law who had not been arrested by states with a "force bill."
"Sharp Knife" and "King Mob" were nicknames of this president, empowered by Congress via the Force Bill

clues.clue: Vice President John C. Calhoun resigned over the Tariff of Abominations and Nullification Crisis
examples: Calhoun served and later resigned as vice president under this 7th President of the United States.

works.name: 1835 Assassination Attempt
works.description: The first attempt to assassinate a sitting U.S. president: house painter Richard Lawrence, believing himself owed money as the rightful King of England, tried to shoot Jackson at a funeral, but both pistols misfired.

clues.clue: Richard Lawrence, a delusional house painter who believed he was the King of England, attempted to shoot Jackson at the funeral of Congressman Warren Davis; both pistols misfired
examples: This mentally delusional house painter, who believed he was a former king of England, unsuccessfully tried to kill President Jackson in 1835.
give this event where, after two pistols misfired, the Democratic president who hated the national bank was not shot.

clues.clue: Jackson beat Lawrence with his cane after the attempt, partly with help from Davy Crockett; Lawrence was later found not guilty by reason of insanity
examples: This politician beat his attempted assassin, Richard Lawrence, with his cane.
the would be assassin Richard Lawrence later successfully used the insanity defense.

works.name: Cabinet Turmoil: Petticoat Affair & Kitchen Cabinet
works.description: Jackson's official cabinet was upended by the "Petticoat affair" — other cabinet wives' social snubbing of Secretary of War John Eaton's wife Peggy — pushing Jackson to rely instead on an informal "Kitchen Cabinet" of advisers.

clues.clue: The Petticoat/Peggy Eaton affair (over the social shunning of John Eaton's wife) upended his cabinet; Lewis Cass replaced Eaton
examples: Lewis Cass replaced John Eaton in the cabinet of this man, which was upended in the Petticoat affair.
Name this president, who endured the much-less interesting Peggy Eaton affair, leading to his unofficial advisement by the Kitchen Cabinet.

clues.clue: Relied on an informal "Kitchen Cabinet" of advisers, including Postmaster General Amos Kendall
examples: Other than postmaster general Amos Kendall, this president mostly ignored the advice of his kitchen cabinet.
This Kentucky-born Postmaster General under Jackson edited the Washington Globe and turned it into the Jackson administration's mouthpiece.

works.name: Rachel Jackson (wife)
works.description: Jackson married Rachel Donelson while her divorce from first husband Lewis Robards was not yet legally final, a scandal political opponents revived as a bigamy accusation during the bitter 1828 campaign. Rachel died shortly after the election, which Jackson blamed on his opponents.

clues.clue: Married Rachel in Natchez before her divorce from Lewis Robards was finalized; the marriage had to be redone once the divorce was official
examples: Her future husband was a boarder at the house of Lewis Robards, whom this woman was married to at the time.
Her marriage in Natchez had to be redone, among other reasons because the territory only recognized Catholic ceremonies at the time.

clues.clue: Political opponents (via a Nashville Committee pamphlet edited by Robert Coleman Foster, with testimony altered by John Overton) attacked her as a bigamist during the 1828 campaign
examples: Named answer required Robert Coleman Foster edited a pamphlet of testimony and narratives about this person's past.
John Overton changed several important dates in testimony about her

clues.clue: Died shortly after the 1828 election; Jackson blamed the attacks on her character for her death
examples: This woman's husband blamed her death on his opponents accusations of bigamy and adultery.

clues.clue: Niece Emily Donelson served as White House hostess after Rachel's death, until dismissed for snubbing Peggy Eaton
examples: Emily Donelson served as hostess for an estate after this woman's death until Donelson was asked to leave for snubbing Peggy Eaton.

works.name: Legacy, Memory & Cultural Depictions
works.description: Jackson's afterlife spans eponymous American cities, an endangered spot on the $20 bill, a contested historiography, ghost folklore tied to his Hermitage, and modern pop-culture treatments.

clues.clue: Multiple U.S. cities are named for him, including Jacksonville, FL (most populous city proper in Florida) and Jackson, MS (state capital, with Jackson Square in New Orleans also bearing his name)
examples: identify this former president who lends his name to the most populous city proper in Florida, as well as the capital of Mississippi.

clues.clue: Portrait on the $20 bill, which the Treasury has proposed replacing with Harriet Tubman
examples: In 2030, the U.S. Treasury will start phasing out currency with this person on it in favor of currency featuring Harriet Tubman.
Mankiller was a candidate to replace this president on the $20 bill.

clues.clue: Subject of major historiography: Arthur Schlesinger's Pulitzer-winning The Age of Jackson, Claude Bowers's Party Battles, Jon Meacham's American Lion, and Charles Sellers's "Market Revolution" thesis
examples: Arthur Schlesinger won his first Pulitzer Prize for a book about this president.
A book by Claude Bowers about the "party battles" was influential in creating his modern image at the slight cost of being often totally made up.

clues.clue: Linked in folklore to the Bell Witch legend and said to haunt the White House Rose Room
examples: The poltergeist-like Bell Witch is said to have literally kicked one of this president's men out of the door. This president's Hermitage Estate in Nashville may still host his ghost.

Ingram's book alleges that this historical figure visited the Bell home in 1819 with a man claiming to be able to kill the witch with a silver bullet. This man's ghost allegedly haunts the Rose Room of the White House.

clues.clue: Title figure of the musical "Bloody Bloody Andrew Jackson"
examples: Name this "Bloody Bloody" president, whose politics are laid out in "Populism, Yea Yea."

clues.clue: Invoked by William Jennings Bryan in the 1896 "Cross of Gold" speech as a populist hero who defeated the moneyed establishment
examples: Bryan claims that, instead of fearing a Robespierre, we instead need this person, whose actions he compares to Cicero's defeat of the Catiline Conspiracy.

comprehensive_summary: Andrew Jackson (1767-1845) was the seventh U.S. president (1829-1837), nicknamed "Old Hickory" and, from his military career, "Sharp Knife." He rose to national fame as a general: defeating William Weatherford's Red Sticks at the Battle of Horseshoe Bend in the Creek War, defending New Orleans against a British invasion in 1815 (later accused in the "Coffin Handbills" of executing six militiamen there), and waging the First Seminole War, during which he court-martialed and executed British subjects Alexander Arbuthnot and Robert Ambrister, sparking a diplomatic incident. He married Rachel Donelson before her divorce from Lewis Robards was legally final, a scandal opponents revived as a bigamy accusation during the bitter 1828 campaign; Rachel died shortly after the election, which Jackson blamed on his political enemies. He won more electoral votes than John Quincy Adams in the contested 1824 election but lost the presidency in a House vote decided by the "corrupt bargain," then defeated Adams in a rematch in 1828 amid newly expanded white male suffrage. As president, his signature policy was the Indian Removal Act, which forced the Cherokee and other southeastern nations onto the Trail of Tears; after the Supreme Court ruled in Worcester v. Georgia that Georgia lacked jurisdiction over Cherokee land, Jackson (reportedly) said "John Marshall has made his decision; now let him enforce it" and declined to intervene. His defining domestic political fight was the "Bank War": he vetoed the recharter of the Second Bank of the United States, clashed with its president Nicholas Biddle, and had federal deposits withdrawn via newly appointed Treasury Secretary Roger Taney, prompting Whig cartoons depicting him as the despotic "King Andrew the First" trampling the Constitution, and as a general "slaying the many-headed monster." His 1836 Specie Circular, requiring gold or silver for public land purchases, is widely blamed for helping trigger the Panic of 1837 shortly after he left office. He also signed the Force Bill during the Nullification Crisis with South Carolina over the "Tariff of Abominations," a fight that led Vice President John C. Calhoun to resign. His official cabinet was upended by the "Petticoat affair" (the social shunning of Secretary of War John Eaton's wife Peggy), pushing Jackson to rely on an informal "Kitchen Cabinet" including Postmaster General Amos Kendall. In 1835 he survived the first assassination attempt on a sitting U.S. president, by delusional house painter Richard Lawrence, whose pistols both misfired; Jackson beat him with his cane. His legacy includes numerous eponymous American cities (Jacksonville, FL; Jackson, MS), a $20 bill portrait slated for replacement by Harriet Tubman, a contested historiography (Schlesinger's The Age of Jackson, Bowers's Party Battles, Meacham's American Lion, Sellers's "Market Revolution" thesis), ghost folklore tied to the Bell Witch legend and his Hermitage estate, and depiction as the title character of the musical Bloody Bloody Andrew Jackson.
recursive_suggestions: Rachel Jackson — has her own tossup answerline (2015 Chicago Open); could merit a standalone topic
Second Bank of the United States — recurring separate answerline (Nicholas Biddle, Bank War) distinct from Jackson himself
Specie Circular — recurring separate bonus-part answerline (3x), tightly linked to the Bank War/Panic of 1837
Worcester v. Georgia — recurring case answerline; watch for confusion with other Marshall Court cases
Nicholas Biddle — president of the Second Bank, Jackson's antagonist in the Bank War
John C. Calhoun — VP under Jackson, Nullification Crisis; also a distinct History topic in his own right (Fort Hill, concurrent majority theory)
Trail of Tears / Indian Removal Act — could be its own Events topic distinct from Jackson the figure
Martin Van Buren — Jackson's successor and VP; Panic of 1837 fell on his watch
Bell Witch — recurring Mythology/folklore answerline linked to Jackson's Hermitage
